ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori prawn; 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302051) is the closest parent class covering prepared crustacean seafood products.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no specific code exists for tandoori-marinated and tandoor-grilled prawn preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for tandoori prawn as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tandoori prawn consists of approximately 6–8 medium-to-large tiger prawns (around 200 g total), marinated in spiced yogurt and cream, grilled in a tandoor oven, and served with mint chutney and sliced onions.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Tandoori Prawn / Grilled Spiced Prawn composite; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central: Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175180) and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for 'tandoori prawn' as a finished dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data and USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (FDC 175180), adjusted upward for fat and energy to account for the yogurt-cream marinade (approximately 5–8% fat contribution from marinade). Protein values are anchored to ICMR-NIN cooked prawn (~20 g/100 g). Sodium is elevated to reflect marinade salt and spice blend. This is a medium-confidence composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate for high-protein low-carbohydrate seafood dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori prawn in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 5 based on the analogous category of high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked seafood dishes (prawns/shrimp, cooked). The University of Sydney GI Database lists cooked shrimp and similar high-protein seafood as having negligible glycemic impact (GI < 10). The small carbohydrate contribution (~3.5 g/100 g) comes from the yogurt-cream marinade and spices. GL per 200 g serving = (5 × 7.0) / 100 = 0.35, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium from marinade salt and spice blend (~520 mg/100 g; ~1040 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 200 g restaurant serving of tandoori prawn contributes approximately 1040 mg sodium (45% of the 2300 mg daily limit), primarily from the marinade salt and spice blend, and should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size accordingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~5) and low carbohydrate content (~3.5 g/100 g) with high protein (~20.5 g/100 g) supporting glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori prawn is a suitable protein-rich, low-GI choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the negligible carbohydrate load (GL ≈ 0.4 per serving) and high protein content support postprandial glycemic stability, though the yogurt-cream marinade adds modest saturated fat that should be considered within overall dietary fat targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol (~155 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g) from cream-yogurt marinade, partially offset by lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, tandoori prawn offers lean high-quality protein and some omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiac health, but the cholesterol content (~310 mg per 200 g serving) and sodium load warrant portion moderation and preference for preparations using low-fat yogurt rather than cream in the marinade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Prawn is a classic North Indian seafood dish in which large tiger prawns are marinated in a spiced mixture of yogurt, cream, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli, and garam masala, then cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The tandoor imparts a characteristic smoky char and slight caramelisation to the prawn shells while keeping the flesh juicy and tender. The dish is typically served with fresh mint chutney, sliced raw onion rings, and lemon wedges. In Singapore, tandoori prawn appears on the menus of Indian restaurants such as Bombay Grill and is a popular starter or sharing dish. Nutritionally, tandoori prawn is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food (~20.5 g protein and ~3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with moderate fat (~5.8 g/100 g), making it one of the more nutritionally favourable Indian restaurant dishes. Its glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ~5), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content is elevated (~520 mg/100 g) due to the marinade, which is a consideration for those with hypertension. The dish provides meaningful amounts of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and zinc from the prawns, and calcium from the yogurt-cream marinade. It is naturally gluten-free and suitable for halal diets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori prawn?
Is tandoori prawn good for diabetics?
Is tandoori prawn high in protein?
Can I eat tandoori prawn if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are in tandoori prawn marinade?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for tandoori prawn as a finished restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data and USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (FDC 175180), with upward adjustment for fat and energy to account for the yogurt-cream marinade. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for high-protein low-carbohydrate cooked seafood. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a composite estimate adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (cooked prawn) and USDA FoodData Central FDC 175180 (shrimp, cooked, moist heat), adjusted for the yogurt-cream tandoori marinade. No exact approved-source entry for tandoori prawn as a finished dish was available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA source entries used for this composite. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries used. 3. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in cooked crustacean preparations per USDA data. 4. GI is estimated at 5 (analogous estimate) based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked seafood; no direct GI measurement for tandoori prawn exists in the approved GI database. 5. The 'Gulzaar Tandoori Prawn' brand name from Bombay Grill (field survey data) is preserved as an alias; the canonical food name is normalised to 'Tandoori Prawn'. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the typical preparation of this dish at Indian Muslim-friendly restaurants in Singapore; kosher status is not confirmed. 7. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for a restaurant portion of 6–8 medium-large tiger prawns; actual restaurant portion weights may vary. 8. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from USDA FDC 175180 and ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data and should be treated as indicative rather than precise.
